context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Peer Assessment (Power/Actor Maps)

Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST for initial submission, plus one additional week for peer review.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week.

Assignment Tasks
Following the completion of your Power/Actors Map, you will continue to complete your Peer Assessment Review of two of your fellow learners' Power/Actor Maps from the ORA assignments in Week 4. Refer to the ORA for instructions on completing the Peer Assessment Review.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, idk what part of this peer assessment i’m even supposed to do rn 😵 can u just explain the whole thing?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you get oriented! Before I explain anything, tell me: have you already looked at the assignment instructions in the ORA (the peer review section)? What have you read so far, and where exactly did you get lost?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation of the assignment, which would essentially do the work of reading and understanding the assignment for them. Instead, I should redirect them to engage with the material themselves and ask a targeted question to help them identify what they already know and what specifically is confusing them. This keeps the interaction Socratic and puts the cognitive work back on the student.

exchanges.student: i skimmed it tbh, but it all blurred together 😩 i just know it says review 2 maps and i’m lost on like… the actual steps.
exchanges.tutor: Good start — you already know the core task. Here's what I'd suggest: go back to the ORA instructions and read them carefully this time, not just skim. Then come back and tell me the steps you find there. What's the first thing the instructions ask you to do?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified one key piece of information (review 2 maps) but hasn't engaged deeply with the instructions. Rather than explaining the steps for them, I should push them to go back to the source material and identify the steps themselves. This is a reading comprehension and engagement issue as much as a content issue.

exchanges.student: ngl i was hoping u could just lay it all out first, bc when i read it i miss stuff. that angle didn’t help lol, so maybe just the big picture + first step?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but honestly the detailed peer review steps live in the ORA itself — I don't have them, so I can't lay them out even if I wanted to. What I can tell you is this: the assignment has two parts — completing your own Power/Actor Map, and then reviewing two classmates' maps. Which of those two parts are you on right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pushing back and asking me to summarize the instructions for them. This would undermine the learning process and essentially do the reading work for them. I should hold my boundary here while being empathetic, and redirect them constructively. I only have access to the assignment description provided to me, which says to refer to the ORA for specific peer review instructions — so I genuinely cannot give them a step-by-step breakdown. I should be honest about that and encourage them to engage with the source.

exchanges.student: the review part i think, but also maybe my own map is prob bad so now i’m confused about both fr. can u just tell me what usually makes a power/actor map “good”?
exchanges.tutor: Let's not get ahead of ourselves — two things at once will just add to the confusion. First, which is it: is your own map submitted already, or not? That determines where we start.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now splitting focus between two tasks and asking a general question about what makes a good power/actor map. This is a bit of a detour — they need to clarify where they actually are in the process before we discuss quality criteria. I should help them focus on one thing at a time, and use a Socratic question to get them to think about what a power/actor map is supposed to show, rather than giving them a checklist of criteria.

exchanges.student: i think i submitted? not 100% tho 😬 if i did, then what am i even looking for when i grade other ppl?
exchanges.tutor: First things first — go check your submission status in the ORA, that's important. While you do that, think about this: what is a Power/Actor map actually supposed to show? Once you can answer that, you'll have a clearer idea of what to look for in someone else's.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking what to look for when reviewing peers' maps. Before I help them think through evaluation criteria, I should push them to first confirm their submission status (a practical step they need to do) and then engage their own thinking about what a Power/Actor map is meant to show. I should ask them a targeted Socratic question about the purpose of the map to get them thinking about evaluation criteria themselves.

exchanges.student: idk, like who matters in the city and who has power maybe? that didn’t really clear it up tbh. what kinds of things make one map strong vs weak?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid starting point — yes, it's about who has power and who matters. Now push that further: why would it matter *how* those actors relate to each other, not just who they are? What would be missing from a map that just listed names?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave a reasonable but vague answer about what a Power/Actor map shows. I should affirm the core idea and then push them to go deeper with a Socratic question, rather than giving them a strong vs. weak criteria list. They need to develop their own thinking here. I'll build on their answer and ask them to extend it slightly.

exchanges.student: maybe bc the connections show influence or conflict? but i’m still fuzzy, can u just give me a simple checklist-ish thing i can use while reviewing?
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely good insight — connections showing influence or conflict is exactly the kind of thing that makes a map more than just a list. Now, you're closer than you think to building your own checklist. Based on what you just said, what would be the first criterion you'd write down?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress with their reasoning — 'connections show influence or conflict' is a strong insight. However, they're asking me to generate a checklist for them, which would be doing the work for them. I should affirm their good thinking and redirect them to build their own checklist from their own reasoning. This keeps the cognitive work with the student.

exchanges.student: maybe “does it show clear relationships,” but idk what else rn. trying a diff angle: can u give me like 3 super basic criteria, not a whole lecture?
exchanges.tutor: Good first criterion. Here's a nudge toward a second: you said a map should show *who* has power — so what would make you confident a map actually captures that, beyond just listing names?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced one good criterion on their own and is now asking me to just give them the rest. I should not do this — it would undermine the learning process. I should affirm their first criterion and use a Socratic question to push them toward a second one, building on the reasoning they've already demonstrated. I'll be brief and direct.

exchanges.student: maybe if it shows levels of power? i’m still blanking on the rest tho 😵
exchanges.tutor: Two solid criteria so far — relationships and levels of power. Think back to the course context: these maps are about climate action in a specific city. What does that suggest a third criterion might be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now generated two solid criteria on their own: relationships and levels of power. I should affirm this progress and push them toward a third using their own earlier reasoning about the city context. We've been on this same idea for several exchanges now, so I should also signal that we're making good progress and nudge them toward integrating what they've built so far.

exchanges.student: maybe if the actors are actually relevant to the city’s climate stuff, not random?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — and that's three strong criteria you've built yourself: relationships between actors, levels of power, and relevance to the city's climate context. That's a real foundation. Now try writing those out in your own words as a short set of notes you could actually use while reviewing. What does that look like when you put it together?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now independently generated three solid criteria: relationships, levels of power, and relevance to the city's climate context. This is great progress and they've done it themselves. Since we've been on this topic for several exchanges and they now have three criteria, I should affirm this and push them to integrate their thinking into a written attempt — perhaps a short set of notes or criteria they can actually use during the peer review. This also aligns with the rule about asking students to integrate after 3+ messages on the same idea.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated strong Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently refusing to hand over answers and instead guiding the student to derive criteria themselves. No near-submission-ready content was provided, key ideas were student-generated, and the tutor maintained a supportive, appropriately concise tone across all turns.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave the student a finished checklist or direct answer; instead, each turn nudged the student to articulate the next criterion themselves (turns 6–10). The student arrived at all three criteria through their own reasoning, with the tutor only confirming and prompting. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor built on each student contribution (e.g., affirming 'relationships' before asking about power levels), and when the student was confused, the tutor asked diagnostic questions rather than dumping information. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback implicitly by modeling how to build criteria from first principles, though explicit method commentary was light; no clear failure threshold was crossed. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation progressed without repetitive cycling or off-topic drift; the tutor kept the student anchored to the assignment task throughout. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise and interactive, tone was warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-implying.
